q: Give prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day for acute PSGN to suppress the immune-mediated glomerular inflammation.
truth: ❌ TRAP — NO steroids in APSGN
exp: APSGN is self-limiting. Steroids are not only ineffective but potentially harmful. Prescribing them in the exam = instant −2 marks.

q: First-line antihypertensive for acute hypertension in AGN is Enalapril (ACE inhibitor).
truth: ❌ TRAP — First-line is Nifedipine (CCB)
exp: ACE inhibitors worsen hyperkalaemia and can precipitate AKI in the setting of low GFR in AGN. First-line = Nifedipine.

q: A child with AGN and BP 150/95 mmHg has mild pedal oedema. Start Furosemide as first-line therapy for the hypertension.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Furosemide = fluid overload only
exp: Furosemide is for fluid overload management, not blood pressure control. HTN → Nifedipine. Fluid overload → Furosemide. Mixing these is a classic −1 mark error.

q: C3 complement is still low at 8 weeks post-PSGN. Reassure the family — this is within the expected recovery range.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Investigate for MPGN
exp: C3 should normalise by 6–8 weeks post-PSGN. Persistent low C3 beyond 8–12 weeks = MPGN until proven otherwise. Reassurance here is dangerous.

q: A child with NS has heavy proteinuria. To reduce urinary losses, restrict protein intake to 0.5 g/kg/day.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Normal protein intake is essential
exp: NS patients are protein-depleted from urinary losses. Restriction worsens malnutrition. Only salt restriction during oedema phase. Protein restriction = −1 mark.

q: A 42 kg child with NS should receive 84 mg/day prednisolone (calculated as 2 mg/kg × 42 kg).
truth: ❌ TRAP — Maximum cap is 60 mg/day
exp: 2 × 42 = 84 mg calculates correctly but the maximum is capped at 60 mg/day regardless of weight. Never ignore the cap.

q: Use ACE inhibitor (Enalapril) as first-line treatment for acute hypertension in nephrotic syndrome.
truth: ❌ TRAP — First-line acute HTN = CCB (Amlodipine)
exp: ACEi are appropriate for persistent HTN/proteinuria (chronic), NOT acute HTN. Acute HTN in NS → CCB (Amlodipine). ACEi for chronic persistent HTN/proteinuria.
marks: Acute vs chronic HTN — the indication matters

q: Give 25% albumin infusion to a stable NS child to help mobilise and treat generalised oedema.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Albumin is for hypovolaemia only
exp: Albumin given for routine oedema (without hypovolaemia) expands plasma volume abruptly → pulmonary oedema. Only use for documented hypovolaemia.

q: Phase 2 of NS steroid protocol is 1 mg/kg alternate days for 6 weeks.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Phase 2 = 1.5 mg/kg alternate days
exp: Phase 1: 2 mg/kg/day × 6 weeks. Phase 2: 1.5 mg/kg alternate days × 6 weeks. Total 12 weeks. 1 mg/kg alternate days is a common confusion — wrong dose.

q: Give insulin 0.1 U/kg IV bolus at the start of DKA management to rapidly bring down blood glucose.
truth: ❌ TRAP — NEVER bolus insulin in paediatric DKA
exp: IV bolus → rapid osmotic shift → cerebral oedema. Only give insulin as a continuous infusion (0.05–0.1 U/kg/hr), started after 1–2 h of fluid resuscitation.

q: Replace the DKA fluid deficit over 24 hours using 0.45% NaCl + KCl.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Deficit replaced over 48 hours
exp: Rapid replacement (24 h) increases the osmotic gradient that drives cerebral oedema. Standard protocol: replace deficit over 48 hours. 24 hours = wrong.
marks: 48 h, NOT 24 h — tested directly in MCQs

q: Blood glucose has normalised in DKA. Stop the insulin infusion now.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Continue insulin until acidosis resolves
exp: Insulin resolves ketoacidosis, not hyperglycaemia. Continue infusion until pH >7.30 AND HCO₃ >18 mEq/L. Stopping at glucose normalisation leaves the child acidotic.

q: Start the insulin infusion simultaneously with IV fluids from the beginning of DKA management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Fluids first for 1–2 hours before insulin
exp: Start 0.9% NS resuscitation first for 1–2 h. Then check K⁺. Then start insulin at 0.05–0.1 U/kg/hr. Starting insulin simultaneously causes rapid osmotic shift.
marks: Sequence matters: fluids → K⁺ check → insulin

q: Severe dehydration, child aged 3 years (Plan C): give Ringer's Lactate 30 mL/kg over 1 hour.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Child 1–5 yr = 30 mL/kg over 30 MIN
exp: 1 hour is for INFANTS (<12 months). Child (1–5 years) = 30 mL/kg over 30 MINUTES. The same volume, different time — age determines the rate.
marks: Most common Plan C error — age-rate trap

q: A 4-month-old with acute diarrhoea needs zinc supplementation. Give zinc 20 mg/day.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Infant <6 months = 10 mg/day
exp: Zinc dosing has an age cutoff at 6 months. <6 months = 10 mg/day. ≥6 months = 20 mg/day. A 4-month-old gets 10 mg/day. 20 mg/day is wrong.
marks: Age cutoff at 6 months — very frequently tested

q: Severe dehydration Plan C: after giving 30 mL/kg bolus, the child is improving. Give the remaining 70 mL/kg over 2.5 hours.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Phase 2 = 70 mL/kg over 5 hours
exp: Phase 2 in Plan C = 70 mL/kg over 5 hours, not 2.5. Halving the time doubles the rate — this is too fast and risks fluid overload.
marks: 70 mL/kg / 5 h — the full Plan C sequence

q: Nutritional rickets treatment: Vitamin D3 2000–4000 IU/day alone is sufficient for full mineralisation.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Elemental calcium is mandatory alongside
exp: Vitamin D increases Ca/PO4 absorption, but you must also supplement elemental calcium 50–100 mg/kg/day. Without calcium, the osteoid cannot mineralise. Vit D alone is insufficient.

q: XLH (familial hypophosphataemia): treat with high-dose cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) to correct the deficiency.
truth: ❌ TRAP — XLH needs phosphate + calcitriol, not Vit D3
exp: XLH is a phosphate-wasting disorder (PHEX/FGF23), not vitamin D deficiency. Treatment: oral phosphate supplements + calcitriol. Cholecalciferol alone = wrong drug target.
marks: XLH ≠ Vit D deficiency — completely different treatment

q: Treatment dose for nutritional rickets: 400 IU/day of Vitamin D3 for 6–12 weeks.
truth: ❌ TRAP — 400 IU/day is prevention, not treatment
exp: 400 IU/day is the prevention/maintenance dose. Treatment requires 2000–4000 IU/day × 6–12 weeks. The doses are 5–10× different. Writing 400 IU for treatment = wrong.

q: X-ray shows white line of Fraenkel at the zone of provisional calcification. Diagnose rickets.
truth: ❌ TRAP — White line of Fraenkel = Scurvy
exp: White line of Fraenkel = Scurvy (vitamin C deficiency). Rickets shows cupping, fraying, and splaying of the metaphysis. These X-ray signs are directly swapped in MCQs.

q: STEC E. coli O157:H7 confirmed in a child with HUS. Start ceftriaxone IV to clear the infection.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Antibiotics are contraindicated in STEC-HUS
exp: Antibiotics (especially fluoroquinolones and beta-lactams) trigger increased Shiga toxin release from dying bacteria → worsens endothelial damage and HUS severity. No antibiotics.
marks: No antibiotics in STEC-HUS — major trap

q: Newborn screening for congenital hypothyroidism should be performed on day 1 of life.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Screening is at day 3–5
exp: Physiological TSH surge occurs at birth. Testing on day 1 gives false-positive results. Optimal screening window: day 3–5 of life (after the surge settles).

q: Crush the levothyroxine tablet and administer it with calcium supplement for better bioavailability.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Calcium reduces levothyroxine absorption
exp: Calcium, soy formula, and iron all significantly reduce levothyroxine absorption. Give separately, at least 4 hours apart. Giving together = subtherapeutic levels.
marks: Soy + iron + calcium all reduce T4 absorption

q: A 3-week-old has congenital hypothyroidism. Expected neonatal features include short stature and intellectual disability.
truth: ❌ TRAP — These are CHILDHOOD features if untreated
exp: In the neonatal period: prolonged jaundice, hypotonia, constipation, large fontanelle, hoarse cry, macroglossia, umbilical hernia. Short stature and ID develop later if untreated.
marks: Examiner explicitly tests neonatal vs childhood features

q: A 14-year-old has sudden severe scrotal pain with absent cremasteric reflex. Wait for Doppler ultrasound result before proceeding to theatre.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Do NOT wait for imaging if suspicion is high
exp: <6 hours = >90% testicular salvage. >24 hours = <10%. Doppler takes time. If clinical suspicion is high (sudden pain + absent cremasteric + normal urine) → go to theatre immediately.
marks: Time is testicle — do not delay for imaging

q: A blue dot sign at the upper pole of the testis is pathognomonic for testicular torsion — emergency surgery is required.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Blue dot sign = torsion of testicular appendage
exp: Blue dot = torsion of the appendix testis (hydatid of Morgagni), NOT testicular torsion. Management is conservative (analgesia, NSAIDs). Testicular torsion has absent cremasteric + no blue dot.
marks: Blue dot ≠ testicular torsion — very different management

q: Child with haematuria 2 days after a throat infection, C3 is normal, C4 is normal. Diagnose PSGN.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Normal C3 + short latent = IgA nephropathy
exp: PSGN: 10–21 day latent period + LOW C3. IgA nephropathy: 1–3 day synpharyngitic onset + NORMAL complement. Normal C3/C4 with haematuria = IgA, not PSGN.

q: Acute variceal bleeding: transfuse to Hb 10–12 g/dL to maintain haemodynamic stability.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Over-transfusion raises portal pressure
exp: Transfusing to Hb 10–12 in variceal bleeding expands blood volume → raises portal venous pressure → dramatically increases rebleeding risk. Target 7–8 g/dL only.
marks: Over-transfusion = rebleed — a genuinely dangerous error

q: Kasai portoenterostomy at 90 days of age gives outcomes equivalent to surgery performed at 60 days.
truth: ❌ TRAP — 90 days has dramatically worse outcomes
exp: 60 days = 50–60% success. 90 days = <20–30% success. The window narrows rapidly after 60 days due to progressive hepatic fibrosis. 90 days is the outer limit, not the equivalent.

q: A child presents with massive proteinuria + hypoalbuminemia + no HTN. Diagnose AGN.
truth: ❌ TRAP — This is NS, not AGN
exp: Confusing AGN with NS — remember: AGN has hematuria + HTN + low C3; NS has massive proteinuria + hypoalbuminemia + no HTN

q: A 7-year-old with AGN and BP 160/100 mmHg. Start Enalapril (ACE inhibitor) as first-line antihypertensive.
truth: ❌ TRAP — First-line is Nifedipine (CCB)
exp: ACE inhibitors worsen hyperkalaemia and can precipitate AKI in the setting of low GFR in AGN. First-line = Nifedipine. This is the single most tested trap.

q: A child with AGN and BP 150/95 mmHg has mild pedal edema. Start Furosemide as first-line therapy for the hypertension.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Furosemide = fluid overload only
exp: Furosemide is for fluid overload management, not blood pressure control. HTN → Nifedipine. Fluid overload → Furosemide. Mixing these is a classic −1 mark error.

q: A 6-year-old with PSGN has persistent gross hematuria and low C3. Give Prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day to suppress the immune-mediated glomerular inflammation.
truth: ❌ TRAP — NO steroids in APSGN
exp: APSGN is self-limiting. Steroids are not only ineffective but potentially harmful. Prescribing them in the exam = instant −2 marks.

q: A 25 kg child with PSGN is discharged without antibiotics. Strep eradication is unnecessary because the infection has already resolved.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Must give Benzathine Penicillin G
exp: Benzathine Penicillin G 600,000 U IM (<27 kg) or 1.2M U IM (≥27 kg) single dose is mandatory for strep eradication. The GN is immune-mediated, but eradication prevents recurrence and spread.

q: A child has haematuria 2 days after a sore throat with normal C3 and normal C4. The diagnosis is PSGN.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Normal C3 + short latent = IgA nephropathy
exp: PSGN: 10–21 day latent period + LOW C3. IgA nephropathy: 1–3 day synpharyngitic onset + NORMAL complement. Normal C3/C4 with haematuria = IgA, not PSGN.

q: Start diuretics as first-line therapy for HTN.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Wrong first-line choice
exp: Using diuretics for HTN — NO; diuretics are only for fluid overload, not HTN control.

q: A 6-year-old with PSGN has persistent gross hematuria. Give Prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day to suppress immune-mediated inflammation.
truth: ❌ TRAP — NO steroids in APSGN
exp: APSGN is self-limiting. Steroids are not only ineffective but potentially harmful. Prescribing them in the exam = instant −2 marks.

q: A 5-year-old has gross haematuria, BP 140/90 mmHg, and low C3 complement. The most likely diagnosis is Nephrotic Syndrome.
truth: ❌ TRAP — This is AGN, not NS
exp: NS has NO haematuria, NO HTN, normal C3. AGN has all three: haematuria + HTN + low C3. Confusing them is a classic examiner trap.

q: First episode NS in a 4-year-old: start Prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day for 6 weeks.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Must be 2 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg)
exp: Phase 1 ISKD protocol = 2 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg/day) × 6 weeks. 1 mg/kg is half the correct dose and will not induce remission.

q: First episode NS responds to steroids by week 4. Stop steroids at 6 weeks total as the disease is cured.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Total 12 weeks for responders
exp: Phase 1: 2 mg/kg/day × 6 weeks. Phase 2: 1.5 mg/kg alternate days × 6 weeks. Total = 12 weeks. Stopping at 6 weeks causes early relapse.

q: A child with NS presents with acute hypertension (BP 150/95 mmHg). Start Enalapril (ACE inhibitor) as first-line antihypertensive.
truth: ❌ TRAP — First-line acute HTN = Amlodipine (CCB)
exp: ACE inhibitors are appropriate for persistent HTN/proteinuria (chronic), NOT acute HTN. Acute HTN in NS → CCB (Amlodipine). ACEi for chronic persistent HTN/proteinuria.

q: A 4-year-old with first-episode NS and generalised oedema. Salt restriction is unnecessary since the child is haemodynamically stable.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Salt restriction is essential
exp: Salt restriction is as important as steroids for oedema control. Without it, oedema persists despite steroids. Never omit salt restriction during the oedema phase.

q: A child with NS and acute hypertension (BP 150/95). Start Enalapril (ACE inhibitor) as first-line antihypertensive.
truth: ❌ TRAP — First-line acute HTN = Amlodipine (CCB)
exp: ACE inhibitors are appropriate for persistent HTN/proteinuria (chronic), NOT acute HTN. Acute HTN in NS → CCB (Amlodipine). ACEi for chronic persistent HTN/proteinuria.

q: For a child with nephrotic syndrome, using ace inhibitors for acute htn in ns.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Using ACE inhibitors for acute HTN in NS
exp: Using ACE inhibitors for acute HTN in NS — use CCBs (Amlodipine) first; ACEi is for persistent HTN/proteinuria.

q: A child with NS and generalised oedema has normal BP. Salt restriction is unnecessary as the child is stable.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Salt restriction is essential
exp: Salt restriction is as important as steroids for oedema control. Without it, oedema persists despite steroids. Never omit salt restriction during the oedema phase.

q: A child with NS has heavy proteinuria. To reduce urinary losses, restrict protein intake to 0.5 g/kg/day.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Normal protein intake is essential
exp: NS patients are protein-depleted from urinary losses. Restriction worsens malnutrition. Only salt restriction during oedema phase. Protein restriction = −1 mark.

q: An X-ray shows white line of Fraenkel at the zone of provisional calcification. Diagnose rickets.
truth: ❌ TRAP — White line of Fraenkel = Scurvy
exp: White line of Fraenkel = Scurvy (vitamin C deficiency). Rickets shows cupping, fraying, and splaying of the metaphysis. These X-ray signs are directly swapped in MCQs.

q: A 2-year-old with nutritional rickets has normal serum calcium. Calcium supplementation is unnecessary since levels are normal.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Elemental calcium is mandatory alongside Vit D
exp: PTH rises first to maintain calcium in early rickets. Vitamin D increases Ca/PO₄ absorption, but you must also supplement elemental calcium 50–100 mg/kg/day. Without calcium, osteoid cannot mineralise.

q: Nutritional rickets treatment: give Vitamin D3 2000–4000 IU/day alone for 6–12 weeks.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Elemental calcium is mandatory alongside
exp: Vitamin D increases Ca/PO₄ absorption, but you must also supplement elemental calcium 50–100 mg/kg/day. Without calcium, the osteoid cannot mineralise. Vit D alone is insufficient.

q: A child with rickets has normal 25(OH)D but low 1,25(OH)₂D. The diagnosis is nutritional vitamin D deficiency.
truth: ❌ TRAP — This is Vit D dependent Type I
exp: Vit D dependent Type I has normal 25(OH)D but low 1,25(OH)₂D due to 1α-hydroxylase deficiency. Nutritional deficiency has LOW 25(OH)D. These are fundamentally different entities with different treatments.

q: A child with rickets has low phosphate but normal calcium and normal 25(OH)D. Treat with high-dose cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3).
truth: ❌ TRAP — XLH needs phosphate + calcitriol
exp: XLH is a phosphate-wasting disorder (PHEX/FGF23), not vitamin D deficiency. Treatment: oral phosphate supplements + calcitriol. Cholecalciferol alone = wrong drug target.

q: Nutritional rickets treatment dose: 400 IU/day of Vitamin D3 for 6–12 weeks.
truth: ❌ TRAP — 400 IU/day is prevention, not treatment
exp: 400 IU/day is the prevention/maintenance dose. Treatment requires 2000–4000 IU/day × 6–12 weeks. The doses are 5–10× different. Writing 400 IU for treatment = wrong.

q: A 2-year-old with rickets has normal serum calcium. Calcium supplementation is unnecessary since levels are normal.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Elemental calcium is mandatory alongside Vit D
exp: PTH rises first to maintain calcium in early rickets. Vitamin D increases Ca/PO₄ absorption, but you must also supplement elemental calcium 50–100 mg/kg/day. Without calcium, osteoid cannot mineralise.

q: A child with rickets has low phosphate but normal calcium and normal 25(OH)D. Treat with high-dose cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3).
truth: ❌ TRAP — XLH needs phosphate + calcitriol
exp: XLH is a phosphate-wasting disorder (PHEX/FGF23), not vitamin D deficiency. Treatment: oral phosphate supplements + calcitriol. Cholecalciferol alone = wrong drug target.

q: Nutritional rickets treatment: give Vitamin D3 2000–4000 IU/day alone for 6–12 weeks without calcium.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Elemental calcium is mandatory alongside
exp: Vitamin D increases Ca/PO₄ absorption, but you must also supplement elemental calcium 50–100 mg/kg/day. Without calcium, the osteoid cannot mineralise. Vit D alone is insufficient.

q: A 3-week-old with congenital hypothyroidism has short stature and intellectual disability as the presenting neonatal features.
truth: ❌ TRAP — These are CHILDHOOD features if untreated
exp: In the neonatal period: prolonged jaundice, hypotonia, constipation, large fontanelle, hoarse cry, macroglossia, umbilical hernia. Short stature and ID develop later if untreated. Examiner explicitly tests neonatal vs childhood features.

q: A newborn with prolonged jaundice has conjugated hyperbilirubinemia. This is the typical pattern in congenital hypothyroidism.
truth: ❌ TRAP — It is UNCONJUGATED hyperbilirubinemia
exp: Congenital hypothyroidism causes prolonged UNCONJUGATED hyperbilirubinemia (not conjugated). This is a high-yield neonatal feature that examiners specifically test.

q: Newborn screening for CH should be performed on day 1 of life immediately after delivery.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Screening is at day 3–5
exp: Physiological TSH surge occurs at birth. Testing on day 1 gives false-positive results. Optimal screening window: day 3–5 of life (after the surge settles).

q: An infant has low T4 with low/normal TSH. Repeat TSH in 2 weeks as this is likely transient.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Low/normal TSH + low T4 = central hypothyroidism
exp: Low/normal TSH with low T4 suggests central (pituitary) hypothyroidism, not primary. Needs MRI pituitary and endocrine referral. Do NOT just wait and repeat.

q: Do not investigate for central hypothyroidism as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing central hypothyroidism is dangerous
exp: Missing central hypothyroidism — low/normal TSH + low T4 = MRI pituitary.

q: Administer levothyroxine together with soy/iron/calcium for better bioavailability.
truth: ❌ TRAP — soy/iron/calcium reduces absorption of levothyroxine
exp: Giving levothyroxine with soy/iron/calcium — reduces absorption.

q: A 14-year-old has gradual onset scrotal pain with fever and pyuria. Diagnose testicular torsion and arrange emergency surgery.
truth: ❌ TRAP — This is epididymo-orchitis, not torsion
exp: Torsion has sudden onset + absent cremasteric + normal urine. Epididymitis has gradual onset + fever + pyuria. Surgery for epididymitis is wrong — needs antibiotics.

q: During surgery for right testicular torsion, after detorsion and confirming viability, fix only the affected testis as the other side is normal.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Must perform bilateral orchidopexy
exp: The anatomical predisposition (bell-clapper deformity) is bilateral in ~50% of cases. Fixing only the affected side leaves the contralateral testis at risk of torsion. Always fix both.

q: Testicular torsion only occurs in adolescents and is not seen in other age groups.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Torsion has TWO peaks: neonates AND adolescents
exp: Torsion occurs in neonates (extravaginal) AND adolescents (intravaginal/bell-clapper). Two distinct peaks. Saying 'only teenagers' loses marks.

q: A 14-year-old has sudden severe scrotal pain with absent cremasteric reflex. Wait for Doppler ultrasound result before proceeding to theatre.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Do NOT wait for imaging if suspicion is high
exp: <6 hours = >90% testicular salvage. >24 hours = <10%. Doppler takes time. If clinical suspicion is high (sudden pain + absent cremasteric + normal urine) → go to theatre immediately.

q: Blue dot sign is pathognomonic for torsion of appendix testis. Emergency surgery is required.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Blue dot sign = testicular torsion, NOT torsion of appendix testis
exp: Blue dot sign = torsion of appendix testis, NOT testicular torsion — different management (conservative).

q: A child with haematuria has clots in the urine. This confirms the bleeding is glomerular in origin.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Clots = NON-glomerular bleeding
exp: Glomerular haematuria does not form clots — RBCs are fragmented and diluted too much. Clots indicate brisk non-glomerular bleeding: stones, tumour, trauma, coagulopathy.

q: A child with haematuria has RBC casts on microscopy. This suggests non-glomerular bleeding.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Clots in urine = non-glomerular. Glomerular hematuria does NOT form clots.
exp: Clots in urine = non-glomerular. Glomerular hematuria does NOT form clots.

q: A child with haematuria: iga nephropathy = normal c3. low c3 points to psgn or mpgn..
truth: ❌ TRAP — IgA nephropathy = normal C3. Low C3 points to PSGN or MPGN.
exp: IgA nephropathy = normal C3. Low C3 points to PSGN or MPGN.

q: A child with haematuria: hypercalciuria is the #1 cause of isolated non-glomerular hematuria in children..
truth: ❌ TRAP — Hypercalciuria is the #1 cause of isolated non-glomerular hematuria in children.
exp: Hypercalciuria is the #1 cause of isolated non-glomerular hematuria in children.

q: A child with haematuria: alport syndrome = x-linked, hearing loss, family history of hematuria + deafness..
truth: ❌ TRAP — Alport syndrome = X-linked, hearing loss, family history of hematuria + deafness.
exp: Alport syndrome = X-linked, hearing loss, family history of hematuria + deafness.

q: A term newborn has blood glucose 25 mg/dL at 1 hour. This is normal and does not need treatment.
truth: ❌ TRAP — <40 mg/dL in term neonate = hypoglycaemia
exp: Term neonate: hypoglycaemia = <40 mg/dL (2.2 mmol/L). Preterm: <30 mg/dL. 25 mg/dL is hypoglycaemia and needs treatment (feed or IV dextrose). Do not dismiss low values.

q: An asymptomatic newborn has glucose 30 mg/dL. No treatment is needed as there are no symptoms.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Asymptomatic hypoglycaemia still needs treatment
exp: Asymptomatic hypoglycaemia still requires treatment (feed or IV dextrose). Silent hypoglycaemia causes brain injury. Symptomatic status does NOT determine treatment need.

q: An IDM newborn has hypoglycaemia due to glycogen depletion.
truth: ❌ TRAP — IDM hypoglycaemia = hyperinsulinism, NOT glycogen depletion
exp: SGA: hypoglycaemia via glycogen depletion + decreased gluconeogenesis. IDM: hypoglycaemia via HYPERINSULINISM (fetal islet cell hyperplasia from maternal hyperglycaemia). Different mechanisms = different management.

q: An asymptomatic newborn with glucose 35 mg/dL is given 2 mL/kg IV dextrose bolus immediately.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Feed first; IV dextrose only if symptomatic or <25 mg/dL
exp: Asymptomatic hypoglycaemia: feed first (breast milk or formula). IV dextrose is for symptomatic babies or those who cannot feed. Avoid unnecessary IV access in stable newborns.

q: A macrosomic newborn with refractory hypoglycemia has no associated congenital anomalies. This is idiopathic hyperinsulinism.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Think Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome
exp: Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome: hyperinsulinism + macrosomia + macroglossia + omphalocele + hemihypertrophy. Always look for associated anomalies in refractory neonatal hypoglycemia.

q: A 5-year-old with intussusception has no lead point because idiopathic intussusception can occur at any age.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Age >3 years implies a lead point
exp: Idiopathic intussusception peaks at 6–36 months. Older children with intussusception likely have a lead point (Meckel, lymphoma, HSP). Always investigate for lead point if age >3 years.

q: Give Contrast enema for symptomatic relief in this patient.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Contrast enema is contraindicated
exp: Contrast enema is contraindicated if there are signs of peritonitis or perforation — surgery first in these cases.

q: Do not investigate for Dance sign (empty RLQ) as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing Dance sign (empty RLQ) is dangerous
exp: Missing Dance sign (empty RLQ) — a subtle but important clinical sign.

q: A 9-month-old has diarrhoea, vomiting, and episodic crying. This is viral gastroenteritis.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Episodic crying + vomiting + sausage mass = intussusception
exp: Gastroenteritis has continuous diarrhoea and no mass. Intussusception has episodic colic (every 15–20 min), vomiting, and a sausage-shaped mass. Confusing them delays reduction and risks bowel necrosis.

q: A 10-month-old with intussusception is observed overnight for spontaneous resolution.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Delayed reduction increases risk of gangrene
exp: Time is bowel. Risk of gangrene increases after 24 hours. Air/contrast enema reduction should be attempted urgently. Observation = bowel loss.

q: In acute variceal bleeding, using selective beta-blockers (metoprolol).
truth: ❌ TRAP — Using selective beta-blockers (metoprolol)
exp: Using selective beta-blockers (metoprolol) — must use non-selective (propranolol, nadolol) to reduce both cardiac output AND splanchnic vasodilation.

q: A child with portal hypertension has abdominal pain. Give ibuprofen for analgesia.
truth: ❌ TRAP — NSAIDs are contraindicated in portal hypertension
exp: NSAIDs worsen bleeding risk (inhibit platelet function) and reduce renal perfusion in cirrhosis. Use paracetamol (safe in liver disease at normal doses) instead. NSAIDs = dangerous error.

q: A child with acute variceal bleeding and Hb 6.5 is transfused to Hb 10–12 g/dL for haemodynamic stability.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Target Hb 7–8 g/dL only
exp: Over-transfusion expands blood volume → raises portal venous pressure → dramatically increases rebleeding risk. Target Hb 7–8 g/dL only. Restrictive transfusion strategy is critical.

q: A patient with acute variceal bleeding has no signs of infection. Antibiotic prophylaxis is unnecessary since there is no active infection.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Antibiotics are MANDATORY in variceal bleed
exp: Ceftriaxone prophylaxis is mandatory in acute variceal bleeding — prevents SBP and reduces mortality. Give even without signs of infection.

q: A child with hematuria, hypertension, and low C3 is diagnosed as pre.
truth: ❌ TRAP — This is hepatorenal syndrome, not pre
exp: Confusing hepatorenal syndrome with pre-renal azotemia — HRS does NOT improve with fluid challenge; pre-renal does.

q: A child with STEC-HUS has ongoing diarrhoea. Start ceftriaxone to clear the E. coli infection.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Antibiotics are contraindicated in STEC-HUS
exp: Antibiotics (especially fluoroquinolones and beta-lactams) trigger increased Shiga toxin release from dying bacteria → worsens endothelial damage and HUS severity. No antibiotics in STEC-HUS.

q: A child with HUS has platelets 15,000/μL and petechiae. Give platelet transfusion to prevent bleeding.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Platelet transfusion is contraindicated in HUS
exp: Platelet transfusion in HUS worsens microvascular thrombosis. Only transfuse if active bleeding or invasive procedure. Low platelets alone is NOT an indication.

q: A child with HUS has fever, neurological symptoms, and severe thrombocytopenia. This is TTP.
truth: ❌ TRAP — TTP is rare in children; this is still HUS
exp: TTP (ADAMTS13 deficiency) is rare in children. HUS can also present with neurological symptoms. The key distinction: HUS = diarrhoea + renal failure + low platelets. TTP = no diarrhoea, ADAMTS13 low.

q: A child with aHUS is started on eculizumab without prior vaccination. Meningococcal vaccine can be given after the first dose.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Vaccinate BEFORE eculizumab
exp: Eculizumab blocks terminal complement (C5). This removes the primary defence against N. meningitidis. Vaccination is mandatory BEFORE starting. No exceptions.

q: A child with HUS has low platelets and microangiopathic changes. Start heparin and aspirin to treat the thrombosis.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Heparin/antiplatelets have no role in typical HUS
exp: Heparin and antiplatelets have no proven benefit in typical STEC-HUS and may worsen bleeding. Supportive care only: fluids, dialysis if needed, BP management.

q: Do not investigate for pneumococcal HUS as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing pneumococcal HUS is dangerous
exp: Missing pneumococcal HUS — occurs after pneumonia/meningitis; different management (antibiotics indicated, not contraindicated).

q: For a jaundiced infant, calling it "physiological jaundice" beyond 14 days.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Calling it "physiological jaundice" beyond 14 days
exp: Calling it "physiological jaundice" beyond 14 days — ANY jaundice >14 days in a breastfed infant or >21 days in a formula-fed infant is pathological and needs workup.

q: Do not investigate for conjugated vs unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing conjugated vs unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia is dangerous
exp: Missing conjugated vs unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia — dark urine and pale stools = conjugated = obstructive = biliary atresia or neonatal hepatitis.

q: A jaundiced infant at 75 days of age is referred for Kasai portoenterostomy. Outcomes are equivalent to surgery at 45 days.
truth: ❌ TRAP — 60 days is the critical cutoff
exp: 60 days = 50–60% success rate. 90 days = <20–30% success. The window narrows rapidly after 60 days due to progressive hepatic fibrosis. Delay = worse prognosis.

q: A jaundiced infant with biliary atresia has isolated liver disease without any associated congenital anomalies.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Check for BASM syndrome
exp: BASM syndrome associations: polysplenia, situs inversus, malrotation, cardiac defects, preduodenal portal vein. Always look for associated anomalies in biliary atresia.

q: A jaundiced infant at 6 weeks has conjugated hyperbilirubinemia. This is neonatal hepatitis; observation is appropriate.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Must rule out biliary atresia promptly
exp: Both biliary atresia and neonatal hepatitis present with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia. HIDA scan and liver biopsy are needed to differentiate. Delay in diagnosing BA beyond 60 days is catastrophic.

q: A jaundiced infant undergoing Kasai portoenterostomy has normal coagulation. Vitamin K supplementation is unnecessary pre-operatively.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Vitamin K is essential pre-Kasai
exp: Coagulopathy from vitamin K deficiency is common in biliary atresia due to malabsorption. Must correct with vitamin K before surgery to prevent bleeding.

q: A child with DKA is given 0.1 U/kg regular insulin IV bolus before starting the continuous infusion.
truth: ❌ TRAP — NEVER bolus insulin in paediatric DKA
exp: IV bolus → rapid osmotic shift → cerebral oedema. Only give insulin as a continuous infusion (0.05–0.1 U/kg/hr), started after 1–2 h of fluid resuscitation.

q: Start insulin before fluids in the management protocol.
truth: ❌ TRAP — fluids must come first
exp: Starting insulin before fluids — insulin drives K+ intracellularly; if dehydrated, this worsens hypovolemia and can cause shock. Give fluids FIRST for 1-2 hours.

q: A child with DKA has pH 7.0. Give sodium bicarbonate IV to correct the acidosis rapidly.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Bicarbonate is contraindicated unless pH <6.9 with shock
exp: Bicarbonate can worsen cerebral oedema and cause paradoxical CNS acidosis. AVOID unless pH <6.9 with haemodynamic instability. Insulin + fluids correct acidosis safely.

q: During DKA management, stopping insulin when glucose normalizes.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Stopping insulin when glucose normalizes
exp: Stopping insulin when glucose normalizes — continue insulin until acidosis resolves (pH >7.30, HCO3 >18), adding dextrose to prevent hypoglycemia.

q: A child with DKA has serum K⁺ 4.5 mEq/L. Potassium supplementation is unnecessary as levels are normal.
truth: ❌ TRAP — DKA patients are TOTAL BODY K⁺ depleted
exp: Despite normal/high serum K⁺, DKA patients are TOTAL BODY K⁺ depleted due to osmotic diuresis and acidosis. Add K⁺ once urine output is confirmed and K⁺ <5.5.

q: A child with DKA is given 0.9% NS 20 mL/kg boluses repeatedly to achieve rapid rehydration over 12 hours.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Replace deficit over 48 hours, not 24
exp: Rapid replacement (24 h) increases the osmotic gradient that drives cerebral oedema. Standard protocol: replace deficit over 48 hours. 24 hours = wrong and dangerous.

q: A child with diarrhoea is given standard WHO ORS with 90 mEq/L sodium.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Low-osmolarity ORS has 75 mEq/L sodium
exp: Low-osmolarity ORS (75 mEq/L Na⁺, 245 mOsm/L) replaced the old standard ORS (90 mEq/L). Using old ORS increases risk of hypernatraemia. Know the current formulation.

q: A 10-month-old with acute diarrhoea is managed with ORS alone. Zinc supplementation is unnecessary as the child is already improving.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Zinc is standard of care
exp: Zinc 20 mg/day (≥6 months) or 10 mg/day (<6 months) for 10–14 days reduces duration and severity of diarrhoea. Always give zinc — it is standard of care.

q: A child with watery diarrhoea and no blood is given azithromycin to reduce stool frequency.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Antibiotics have no role in viral diarrhoea
exp: Most childhood diarrhoea is viral (rotavirus, norovirus). Antibiotics do not reduce duration, increase resistance, and can cause complications. Only give antibiotics for dysentery or cholera.

q: A 2-year-old with acute diarrhoea is given loperamide to reduce stool frequency.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Antimotility agents are contraindicated in children
exp: Antimotility agents (loperamide) are contraindicated in children <2 years and in dysentery. They prolong bacterial excretion, cause ileus, and can precipitate toxic megacolon.

q: A 6-month-old with diarrhoea is kept nil per oral for 6 hours to rest the gut.
truth: ❌ TRAP — NEVER stop breastfeeding; continue feeding
exp: Never stop breastfeeding during diarrhoea. Continue age-appropriate diet throughout. Fasting worsens malnutrition and prolongs recovery. Early refeeding is key.

q: A child with severe dehydration (Plan C) is given 0.9% Normal Saline as the resuscitation fluid.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Ringer's Lactate is preferred over NS
exp: Ringer's Lactate is preferred for Plan C because lactate buffers acidosis. NS can cause hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis. LR = preferred; NS = acceptable but not ideal.

q: A child with hypernatraemic dehydration (Na⁺ 158) is given rapid free water replacement to correct sodium over 12 hours.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Correct hypernatraemia slowly over 48h
exp: Rapid correction of hypernatraemia causes cerebral oedema and seizures. Correct slowly over 48 hours using 0.45% NS or ORS. Max rate: 0.5 mEq/L/hr. Fast correction = brain damage.

q: A child with fever and cloudy urine has leukocyte esterase and nitrite positive on dipstick. Start antibiotics without urine culture.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Dipstick alone is insufficient; need urine culture
exp: Dipstick has false positives (contamination, vulvovaginitis) and false negatives. Urine culture is mandatory for definitive diagnosis and antibiotic sensitivity. Never treat UTI based on dipstick alone.

q: Do not investigate for nonspecific presentation in infants as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing nonspecific presentation in infants is dangerous
exp: Missing nonspecific presentation in infants — fever without source in <2 years = UTI until proven otherwise.

q: A 5-year-old with cystitis is given cotrimoxazole 5 mg/kg BD for 3 days.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Cotrimoxazole dose is 6 mg/kg TMP component BD
exp: Cotrimoxazole dose is 6 mg/kg of the trimethoprim (TMP) component BD. 5 mg/kg is subtherapeutic. Underdosing leads to treatment failure and resistance.

q: A child with recurrent UTIs and grade III VUR has no need for antibiotic prophylaxis since the infections are being treated appropriately.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Prophylaxis is indicated for recurrent UTI/VUR
exp: Nightly low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis (Nitrofurantoin or Cotrimoxazole) for 3–6 months is indicated for recurrent UTIs and moderate-severe VUR.

q: An 18-month-old with first febrile UTI has normal renal function. VUR investigation is not indicated at this age.
truth: ❌ TRAP — USG KUB + MCU indicated after first febrile UTI in <2 years
exp: All children <2 years with first febrile UTI need USG KUB + MCU to rule out VUR and structural anomalies. Omitting investigation is a major error.

q: A 3-year-old with febrile UTI and loin pain is given nitrofurantoin for 7 days.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Nitrofurantoin does NOT achieve renal tissue levels
exp: Nitrofurantoin is concentrated in urine but does NOT achieve therapeutic levels in renal tissue. It is ONLY for lower UTI (cystitis). Pyelonephritis needs ceftriaxone, cefixime, or cotrimoxazole.

q: A child is diagnosed with VUR. Sibling screening is unnecessary as VUR is not familial.
truth: ❌ TRAP — 30% of siblings have VUR
exp: VUR has 30% prevalence in siblings. All siblings should be screened with USG KUB + MCU. Saying it is not familial is incorrect.

q: A child is diagnosed with primary VUR. Sibling screening is unnecessary as this is an isolated case.
truth: ❌ TRAP — 30% of siblings have VUR
exp: VUR has 30% prevalence in siblings. All siblings should be screened with USG KUB + MCU. Saying it is not familial is incorrect.

q: A 2-month-old with VUR is started on nitrofurantoin prophylaxis.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Nitrofurantoin is contraindicated <3 months
exp: Nitrofurantoin is contraindicated in infants <3 months due to risk of haemolytic anaemia. Use cotrimoxazole or cephalexin instead. Age-appropriate antibiotic choice is critical.

q: A child with VUR undergoes DMSA to diagnose VUR.
truth: ❌ TRAP — MCU diagnoses VUR; DMSA detects scarring
exp: MCU (micturating cystourethrogram) diagnoses VUR by showing reflux of contrast up the ureters. DMSA detects renal scarring and function. Do NOT use DMSA to diagnose VUR.

q: A 2-year-old with grade II VUR is scheduled for ureteric reimplantation.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Most VUR resolves spontaneously; surgery is last resort
exp: Most VUR (grades I–III) resolves spontaneously with growth. Surgery (ureteric reimplantation or endoscopic injection) is reserved for persistent high-grade VUR (IV–V), breakthrough infections, or progressive renal damage.

q: Do not investigate for secondary VUR causes as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing secondary VUR causes is dangerous
exp: Missing secondary VUR causes — PUV in males, neurogenic bladder, constipation.

q: A child with metabolic acidosis, hypokalemia, and alkaline urine is diagnosed with diarrhoea-induced metabolic acidosis.
truth: ❌ TRAP — This is RTA, not diarrhoea
exp: Diarrhoea causes metabolic acidosis with acid urine (appropriate compensation). RTA causes metabolic acidosis with INAPPROPRIATELY alkaline urine (urine pH >5.5). The urine pH distinguishes them.

q: A child with RTA has hyperkalemia. This is consistent with Type I or Type II RTA.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Hyperkalemia = Type IV RTA
exp: Type I and II RTA cause hypokalemia (due to renal K⁺ wasting). Type IV RTA causes HYPERkalemia (aldosterone deficiency/resistance). This distinction is frequently tested.

q: A child with Type IV RTA and hyperkalemia is given potassium citrate to correct acidosis.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Potassium citrate worsens hyperkalemia in Type IV
exp: Type IV RTA causes hyperkalemia. Giving potassium citrate worsens hyperkalemia. Use sodium bicarbonate + fludrocortisone. Potassium citrate is for hypokalaemic RTA (Types I and II).

q: Do not investigate for Fanconi syndrome association with Type II as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing Fanconi syndrome association with Type II is dangerous
exp: Missing  Fanconi syndrome association with Type II — glycosuria + aminoaciduria + phosphaturia + RTA = Fanconi.

q: A child with Type II RTA needs 1–2 mEq/kg/day alkali because proximal bicarbonate wasting is mild.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Type II RTA requires 5–15 mEq/kg/day alkali
exp: Type II RTA requires 5–15 mEq/kg/day alkali (much higher than Type I: 1–2 mEq/kg/day) because of massive HCO₃⁻ wasting. Underdosing = persistent acidosis and growth failure.

q: Do not investigate for retractile testis as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing retractile testis is dangerous
exp: Missing retractile testis — this is NORMAL and does NOT need surgery. Differentiate by whether testis stays down after traction.

q: Delay orchidopexy until after 12 months — outcomes are equivalent.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Must not delay beyond 12 months
exp: Delaying orchidopexy beyond 12 months — germ cell loss begins at 6-12 months; earlier surgery preserves fertility.

q: A newborn has bilateral non-palpable testes. No further workup is needed as this is likely just bilateral cryptorchidism.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Must rule out DSD (check karyotype + 17-OHP)
exp: Bilateral non-palpable testes requires DSD workup: karyotype, 17-OHP, pelvic USS. Could be CAH (46,XX DSD) or testicular agenesis. Never assume simple cryptorchidism.

q: After orchidopexy, inform the family that malignancy risk is no longer a concern.
truth: ❌ TRAP — orchidopexy does not eliminate malignancy risk
exp: Thinking orchidopexy eliminates malignancy risk — risk is reduced but NOT eliminated; lifelong self-examination needed.

q: A newborn has unilateral undescended testis. Perform orchidopexy immediately within the first month of life.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Optimal timing is 6–12 months
exp: Germ cell loss begins at 6–12 months. Orchidopexy at 6–12 months preserves fertility. Neonatal surgery is too early; delaying to childhood is too late.

q: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia is assigned male sex immediately at birth based on phallic size alone.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Never assign sex without full DSD workup
exp: Sex assignment requires full DSD workup: karyotype, pelvic USS, 17-OHP, androgen levels, and multidisciplinary team input. Assigning sex prematurely can have devastating psychosocial consequences.

q: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia has normal electrolytes. 17-OHP testing is unnecessary as there is no salt-wasting crisis.
truth: ❌ TRAP — ALWAYS check 17-OHP in ambiguous genitalia
exp: 21-hydroxylase deficiency (CAH) accounts for 95% of ambiguous genitalia cases. 17-OHP is elevated in CAH. Always check regardless of electrolyte status.

q: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia has bilateral palpable gonads in the labioscrotal folds. The karyotype is most likely 46,XX.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Palpable gonads = testes → 46,XY DSD
exp: Ovaries do not descend. Palpable gonads in the labioscrotal folds are testes. This points to 46,XY DSD (not 46,XX). Karyotype will confirm.

q: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia has normal electrolytes. 17-OHP testing is unnecessary as there is no salt-wasting crisis.
truth: ❌ TRAP — ALWAYS check 17-OHP in ambiguous genitalia
exp: 21-hydroxylase deficiency (CAH) accounts for 95% of ambiguous genitalia cases. 17-OHP is elevated in CAH. Always check regardless of electrolyte status.

q: A newborn with mild hypospadias is scheduled for circumcision before completing the DSD workup.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Never perform circumcision before DSD diagnosis
exp: Circumcision before DSD diagnosis is contraindicated. The foreskin may be needed for reconstruction (e.g., in severe hypospadias). Always complete DSD workup first.

q: A child with AKI has low urine sodium and FENa <1%. This confirms intrinsic renal failure.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Low FENa = pre-renal, NOT intrinsic
exp: Pre-renal: low urine Na⁺ (<20), FENa <1% (kidneys conserving Na⁺). Intrinsic: high urine Na⁺ (>40), FENa >2% (tubular damage). Confusing these = wrong management.

q: A child with AKI has pain and fever. Give ibuprofen for symptomatic relief.
truth: ❌ TRAP — NSAIDs are contraindicated in AKI
exp: NSAIDs reduce renal blood flow by inhibiting prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriolar dilation. In AKI, this worsens renal perfusion and can precipitate dialysis. Use paracetamol instead.

q: A child with intrinsic AKI and pulmonary oedema is given aggressive IV fluid resuscitation to improve renal perfusion.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Fluid resuscitation in intrinsic AKI with fluid overload is dangerous
exp: Intrinsic AKI with fluid overload (pulmonary oedema) needs fluid RESTRICTION + diuretics/dialysis, not resuscitation. Giving fluids worsens oedema and can cause respiratory failure.

q: Do not investigate for PUV as a cause of post-renal AKI in male infants as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing PUV as a cause of post-renal AKI in male infants is dangerous
exp: Missing PUV as a cause of post-renal AKI in male infants — weak stream, palpable bladder, hydronephrosis.

q: A child with AKI has K⁺ 6.2, refractory fluid overload, and uraemic encephalopathy. Continue medical management and delay dialysis as the child is stable.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Dialysis is indicated NOW
exp: Dialysis indications: refractory fluid overload, K⁺ >6.5, severe acidosis (pH <7.1), uraemic encephalopathy/pericarditis. Delaying dialysis in these settings is dangerous.

q: Do not investigate for salt-wasting crisis at 7-14 days as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing salt-wasting crisis at 7-14 days is dangerous
exp: Missing salt-wasting crisis at 7-14 days — virilized female with vomiting and shock = CAH emergency; start steroids immediately.

q: An infant with salt-wasting CAH is started on dexamethasone as glucocorticoid replacement.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Hydrocortisone is first-line in infants
exp: Dexamethasone is too potent and has a long half-life — risks growth suppression in infants. Hydrocortisone 10–15 mg/m²/day is first-line in infants and children. Dexamethasone is for adults only.

q: A child with CAH is stable on maintenance hydrocortisone. Emergency injectable hydrocortisone is unnecessary for home use.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Parents MUST have emergency hydrocortisone
exp: Parents MUST have injectable hydrocortisone for sick-day rules and adrenal crisis. Delay in treatment during illness can be fatal. Every family needs an emergency kit.

q: A child with CAH has frequent illness and poor growth. Increase the hydrocortisone dose to suppress all symptoms.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Overtreatment causes iatrogenic Cushing syndrome
exp: Overtreatment with glucocorticoids causes iatrogenic Cushing syndrome, growth suppression, and obesity. The goal is minimum effective dose. Poor growth + obesity = overtreatment, not undertreatment.

q: Do not investigate for 11β-hydroxylase deficiency as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing 11β-hydroxylase deficiency is dangerous
exp: Missing 11β-hydroxylase deficiency — causes hypertension (excess 11-deoxycorticosterone) rather than salt-wasting.

q: A child with suspected Wilson's disease has low ceruloplasmin. This confirms the diagnosis.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Ceruloplasmin alone is insufficient
exp: Ceruloplasmin is low in 90% of Wilson's but also low in other liver diseases and Menkes disease. Confirm with: serum copper, 24h urinary copper (>100 μg/day), liver copper (>250 μg/g dry weight), Kayser-Fleischer rings.

q: A child with Wilson's disease is started on Penicillamine without pyridoxine. Vitamin B6 is not needed as it is not part of the treatment regimen.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Pyridoxine is mandatory with penicillamine
exp: Penicillamine is anti-pyridoxine and can cause neuropathy. Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) must be given concurrently. Omitting it risks peripheral neuropathy.

q: A 12-year-old with symptomatic Wilson's disease and hepatitis is started on zinc monotherapy.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Zinc is for maintenance/preventive, NOT initial symptomatic therapy
exp: Zinc is used for maintenance or in presymptomatic siblings. Symptomatic patients need chelation (D-penicillamine or trientine) first. Zinc monotherapy in symptomatic patients is inadequate and dangerous.

q: Do not investigate for Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia is dangerous
exp: Missing Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia — acute hemolysis + liver failure = Wilson disease until proven otherwise.

q: A child is diagnosed with Wilson's disease. Sibling screening is unnecessary as this is a sporadic mutation.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Autosomal recessive; ALL siblings need evaluation
exp: Wilson's disease is autosomal recessive (ATP7B mutation). All siblings have a 25% chance of being affected and 50% chance of being carriers. Must screen all siblings.

q: A child with hematuria, hypertension, and low C3 is diagnosed as anti.
truth: ❌ TRAP — This is anti-HBc, not anti
exp: Confusing anti-HBc with anti-HBs — Anti-HBc indicates past/present infection; Anti-HBs indicates immunity. Vaccine produces anti-HBs but NOT anti-HBc.

q: Do not investigate for HBIG within 12 hours for infants of HBsAg+ mothers as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing HBIG within 12 hours for infants of HBsAg+ mothers is dangerous
exp: Missing HBIG within 12 hours for infants of HBsAg+ mothers — delayed administration reduces efficacy dramatically.

q: A newborn of an HBsAg+ mother is breastfed without HBIG/vaccine. The risk of chronic infection is only 10%.
truth: ❌ TRAP — 90% of perinatally infected infants become chronic carriers
exp: Without prophylaxis, 90% of perinatally infected infants become chronic carriers (vs 5% in adults). This is why HBIG + vaccine within 12 hours is critical.

q: A 3-year-old with chronic hepatitis B is started on interferon-alpha as first-line therapy.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Interferon is NOT first-line in children
exp: Interferon is poorly tolerated in children and not first-line. Preferred in children: entecavir or tenofovir (oral, well-tolerated). Interferon is reserved for selected adults.

q: Give Thinking breastfeeding for symptomatic relief in this patient.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Thinking breastfeeding is contraindicated
exp: Thinking breastfeeding is contraindicated — breastfeeding is safe unless nipples are cracked/bleeding.

q: A child with hematuria, hypertension, and low C3 is diagnosed as Gilbert syndrome.
truth: ❌ TRAP — This is CN, not Gilbert syndrome
exp: Confusing CN with Gilbert syndrome — Gilbert has UGT1A1 partial deficiency but bilirubin <6 mg/dL, adult onset, no kernicterus.

q: A child with Crigler-Najjar syndrome shows >25% reduction in bilirubin with phenobarbital. This confirms Type I disease.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Phenobarbital response = Type II, NOT Type I
exp: Type I has NO response to phenobarbital (complete UGT1A1 deficiency). Type II shows >25% reduction (partial deficiency). Response to phenobarbital distinguishes them.

q: Do not investigate for liver transplantation as definitive cure for Type I as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing liver transplantation as definitive cure for Type I is dangerous
exp: Missing liver transplantation as definitive cure for Type I — do NOT delay; kernicterus is irreversible.

q: A child with Crigler-Najjar Type I is managed with phototherapy as definitive treatment.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Phototherapy is palliative; liver transplant is definitive for Type I
exp: Phototherapy is palliative in both types. Type I (complete UGT1A1 deficiency) requires liver transplant for cure. Type II (partial deficiency) responds to phenobarbital. Do not call phototherapy 'definitive'.

q: A child with Crigler-Najjar Type II has mild jaundice. No dietary precautions are needed as the condition is mild and stable.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Fasting and illness can precipitate kernicterus even in Type II
exp: Type II is milder but fasting, illness, and stress can precipitate bilirubin rise and kernicterus risk. Families must be warned about these triggers.

q: A teenager with paracetamol overdose has no symptoms at 4 hours. NAC can be delayed until symptoms develop.
truth: ❌ TRAP — NAC is most effective within 8 hours; do NOT wait for symptoms
exp: Paracetamol toxicity is asymptomatic for 24 hours. Waiting for symptoms delays treatment past the 8-hour window when NAC is most effective. Start NAC based on serum level or time of ingestion, NOT symptoms.

q: Do not investigate for the 8-hour window as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing the 8-hour window is dangerous
exp: Missing the 8-hour window — NAC is most effective if given within 8 hours; do NOT delay for charcoal or lab results if >8 hours since ingestion.

q: A 30 kg child receives NAC 150 mg/kg orally as the loading dose.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Oral loading dose is 140 mg/kg
exp: Oral NAC: 140 mg/kg loading dose, then 70 mg/kg every 4 hours × 17 doses. IV NAC: 150 mg/kg over 1h, then 50 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h. Do not confuse oral and IV dosing.

q: A 3-year-old ingested 20 paracetamol tablets 30 minutes ago. Gastric decontamination is unnecessary as the child is asymptomatic.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Give activated charcoal within 1–2 hours
exp: Activated charcoal 1 g/kg (max 50 g) reduces absorption by 50–90% if given within 1–2 hours. Asymptomatic status does NOT negate the need for decontamination.

q: A child with paracetamol poisoning has INR 2.5. Give FFP prophylactically to correct coagulopathy.
truth: ❌ TRAP — FFP is only for active bleeding
exp: FFP only for active bleeding — NOT for INR correction alone. INR is a prognostic marker (King's College criteria). Correcting INR with FFP masks prognostic value and does not improve outcomes.

q: A child with suspected coeliac disease has tTG IgA 15× ULN and positive EMA. Start gluten-free diet immediately without biopsy.
truth: ❌ TRAP — ESPGHAN biopsy-free criteria require tTG >10× ULN + positive EMA
exp: ESPGHAN 2012 criteria allow serology-only diagnosis ONLY if tTG >10× ULN AND positive EMA AND symptomatic. Even then, biopsy remains gold standard in most settings. Do not skip biopsy routinely.

q: Do not investigate for IgA deficiency as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing IgA deficiency is dangerous
exp: Missing IgA deficiency — always check total serum IgA; if deficient, use IgG-based tests (DGP IgG, tTG IgG).

q: Start GFD before completing investigations in the management protocol.
truth: ❌ TRAP — completing investigations must come first
exp: Starting GFD before completing investigations — serology and biopsy require ongoing gluten exposure for accuracy.

q: A child with coeliac disease can safely eat any brand of oats as part of a gluten-free diet.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Only pure, uncontaminated oats are safe
exp: Pure oats are safe in coeliac disease, but most commercial oats are cross-contaminated with wheat during processing. Only oats labelled 'gluten-free' are safe. Regular oats = gluten exposure.

q: A child with coeliac disease is started on a gluten-free diet. No additional vaccinations are needed beyond the routine schedule.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Coeliac causes hyposplenism; extra vaccines needed
exp: Coeliac disease causes hyposplenism. Extra vaccines needed: pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib, annual influenza. This is often forgotten in exam answers.

q: A child with a classic renal mass and lung metastases undergoes percutaneous biopsy before nephrectomy.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Biopsy is contraindicated in classic Wilms tumor
exp: Biopsy is contraindicated in classic Wilms tumor as it upstages the tumour (spillage = stage III). Diagnosis is clinical/radiological. Nephrectomy is both diagnostic and therapeutic. Biopsy only if atypical.

q: A child with Wilms' tumor has normal coagulation studies. No bleeding risk is anticipated during nephrectomy.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Wilms' tumor causes acquired vWD
exp: Wilms' tumor absorbs vWF causing acquired von Willebrand disease. Check coagulation before surgery. May need DDAVP or vWF concentrate perioperatively.

q: A child with hematuria, hypertension, and low C3 is diagnosed as neuroblastoma.
truth: ❌ TRAP — This is Wilms tumor, not neuroblastoma
exp: Confusing Wilms tumor with neuroblastoma — Wilms is intrarenal, smooth, does NOT cross midline; neuroblastoma is extrarenal, irregular, often crosses midline, causes periorbital ecchymosis and opsoclonus-myoclonus.

q: Do not investigate for hypertension as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing hypertension is dangerous
exp: Missing hypertension — 25% of Wilms tumor patients have HTN due to renin secretion.

q: A child with unilateral Wilms' tumor is scheduled for nephrectomy. The contralateral kidney does not need imaging as the tumor is unilateral.
truth: ❌ TRAP — 5–10% are bilateral; ALWAYS image both kidneys
exp: 5–10% of Wilms' tumors are bilateral. Must image the contralateral kidney preoperatively with CT/MRI. Missing bilateral disease changes surgical approach.

q: Do not investigate for failure to pass meconium >48 hours as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing failure to pass meconium >48 hours is dangerous
exp: Missing failure to pass meconium >48 hours — this is the hallmark in neonates.

q: A sick neonate with Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis is scheduled for barium enema to confirm the diagnosis.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Barium enema is contraindicated in enterocolitis
exp: Barium enema in enterocolitis risks perforation and barium peritonitis. Use water-soluble contrast or postpone imaging until stable. Biopsy is the gold standard anyway.

q: A child with chronic constipation and normal rectal biopsy is diagnosed with Hirschsprung disease.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Normal biopsy rules OUT Hirschsprung
exp: Rectal suction biopsy showing absent ganglion cells is diagnostic of Hirschsprung. Normal biopsy rules it OUT. Do not diagnose Hirschsprung with a normal biopsy.

q: A newborn has not passed meconium at 72 hours and has abdominal distension. This is functional constipation and will resolve with glycerine suppositories.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Failure to pass meconium >48h = Hirschsprung until proven otherwise
exp: Hirschsprung: failure to pass meconium >48h, abdominal distension, tight empty rectum. Functional constipation: normal meconium passage, palpable stool in rectum. Confusing them delays critical diagnosis.

q: A newborn with Hirschsprung's disease has no associated chromosomal abnormalities. Genetic testing is unnecessary.
truth: ❌ TRAP — 5–10% have Down syndrome
exp: 5–10% of Hirschsprung patients have trisomy 21. Always look for associated anomalies and consider karyotype if dysmorphic features are present.

q: A child with chronic hyponatremia (Na⁺ 118) is corrected to Na⁺ 138 over 12 hours using 3% saline.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Max correction 8–10 mEq/L in 24h
exp: Rapid correction >12 mEq/L/24h causes central pontine myelinolysis (locked-in syndrome, quadriplegia). Max = 8–10 mEq/L/24h for chronic hyponatraemia. Slow and steady.

q: A child with hyponatremia after neurosurgery is diagnosed with SIADH and placed on fluid restriction.
truth: ❌ TRAP — CSW is hypovolaemic; fluid restriction causes SHOCK
exp: CSW (cerebral salt wasting) causes hypovolaemic hyponatraemia with high urine Na⁺. Fluid restriction causes SHOCK. CSW needs aggressive NaCl + fludrocortisone. SIADH needs fluid restriction.

q: A child with euvolemic hyponatremia has normal cortisol and normal TSH. SIADH is the only cause to consider.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Must rule out adrenal insufficiency and hypothyroidism
exp: Adrenal insufficiency and hypothyroidism are important causes of euvolemic hyponatremia. Must check cortisol and TSH before diagnosing SIADH. Missing these is a common error.

q: A child with SIADH and Na⁺ 128 is given 0.9% Normal Saline at maintenance rate.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Normal saline may WORSEN SIADH
exp: Normal saline is hypotonic relative to urine in SIADH (urine osmolality >300). May worsen hyponatraemia. Use fluid restriction or hypertonic saline. Normal saline ≠ treatment for SIADH.

q: Do not investigate for hyperglycemia as cause of pseudohyponatremia as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing hyperglycemia as cause of pseudohyponatremia is dangerous
exp: Missing hyperglycemia as cause of pseudohyponatremia — correct measured Na⁺ by adding 1.6 mEq/L for each 100 mg/dL glucose above 100.

q: A newborn with CDH is taken to theatre immediately upon diagnosis for emergency hernia repair.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Stabilise first (gentle ventilation, NG, inotropes)
exp: CDH management: stabilise first with gentle ventilation, NG decompression, inotropes, and pulmonary vasodilators. Surgery is delayed until haemodynamically stable. Rush to surgery = high mortality.

q: A newborn with CDH and respiratory distress is bag-mask ventilated aggressively to improve oxygenation.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Bag-mask ventilation causes pneumothorax in CDH
exp: Aggressive bag-mask ventilation forces air into the stomach and herniated bowel, causing pneumothorax and worsening mediastinal shift. Use gentle ventilation, immediate NG decompression, and early intubation.

q: A newborn with CDH is placed on high-pressure conventional ventilation to recruit the hypoplastic lung.
truth: ❌ TRAP — High pressure causes pneumothorax and barotrauma
exp: High-pressure ventilation causes pneumothorax and barotrauma in CDH. Use gentle ventilation (low pressures, permissive hypercapnia), HFOV, or ECMO if needed. Protect the fragile lungs.

q: A newborn with CDH is breathing spontaneously. NG decompression is unnecessary as the baby is not vomiting.
truth: ❌ TRAP — NG decompression is CRITICAL in CDH
exp: NG decompression is critical to decompress bowel herniated into the chest and improve ventilation. Always pass NG immediately in CDH, even if not vomiting.

q: Do not investigate for associated anomalies as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing associated anomalies is dangerous
exp: Missing associated anomalies — 30-40% have other anomalies; always evaluate cardiac and chromosomal status.

q: A child with HRS and ascites is given high-dose furosemide and spironolactone to improve renal perfusion.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Diuretics worsen HRS
exp: Diuretics worsen HRS by further reducing effective circulating volume. Management: volume expansion with albumin, vasoconstrictors (terlipressin, noradrenaline), and TIPS. Diuretics = harmful.

q: A child with cirrhosis and oliguria is given furosemide to improve urine output.
truth: ❌ TRAP — HRS needs volume expansion with albumin, NOT diuretics
exp: Diuretics worsen HRS by further reducing effective circulating volume. Management: volume expansion with albumin, vasoconstrictors (terlipressin, noradrenaline). Diuretics = harmful.

q: A child with cirrhosis and oliguria has acute tubular necrosis as the cause of renal failure.
truth: ❌ TRAP — HRS is functional, not intrinsic renal failure
exp: HRS is functional renal failure due to splanchnic vasodilation and renal vasoconstriction. Kidneys are structurally normal. ATN has granular casts and high FeNa. HRS has low FeNa and no casts.

q: A child with HRS is given low-dose dopamine for renal protection and to improve urine output.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Dopamine has no role in HRS
exp: Low-dose dopamine does not improve outcomes in HRS and is not recommended. Use albumin + vasoconstrictors (terlipressin/noradrenaline) instead. Dopamine = outdated and ineffective.

q: A child with HRS type 1 is managed medically with albumin and terlipressin. Liver transplant evaluation is deferred until medical therapy fails.
truth: ❌ TRAP — HRS = indication for expedited liver transplant
exp: HRS type 1 is an indication for expedited liver transplant evaluation. Medical therapy is a bridge to transplant, not definitive treatment. Do not delay transplant evaluation.

q: Do not investigate for Turner syndrome in short girls as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing Turner syndrome in short girls is dangerous
exp: Missing Turner syndrome in short girls — ALWAYS check karyotype in girls with unexplained short stature.

q: A child with short stature has a single random GH level of 3 ng/mL. This confirms growth hormone deficiency.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Random GH is unreliable; need stimulation test
exp: Random GH is unreliable due to pulsatile secretion. Diagnosis requires TWO stimulation tests (clonidine, arginine, glucagon, insulin tolerance) with peak GH <10 ng/mL. Single random level is meaningless.

q: A child with short stature has normal growth velocity and no GI symptoms. Celiac screening is unnecessary as there are no digestive complaints.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Screen ALL children with unexplained short stature for celiac
exp: Celiac disease is a common cause of short stature even WITHOUT GI symptoms. Screen ALL children with unexplained short stature with tTG IgA + total IgA.

q: Do not investigate for hypothyroidism as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing hypothyroidism is dangerous
exp: Missing hypothyroidism — one of the most treatable causes; always check TSH.

q: A 14-year-old with short stature has low GH, low IGF-1, and midfacial hypoplasia. The diagnosis is constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP).
truth: ❌ TRAP — This is GHD, not CDGP
exp: CDGP has normal GH response, normal IGF-1, family history of delayed puberty. GHD has low GH, low IGF-1, may have midfacial hypoplasia. These are fundamentally different.

q: A child with ESRD from reflux nephropathy is listed for transplant. No urologic evaluation is needed as the native kidneys will be removed.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Must address PUV, VUR, neurogenic bladder before transplant
exp: Posterior urethral valves, VUR, and neurogenic bladder must be addressed before transplant to protect the graft. Urologic evaluation is mandatory.

q: Do not investigate for BK virus nephropathy as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing BK virus nephropathy is dangerous
exp: Missing BK virus nephropathy — causes graft dysfunction 6-12 months post-transplant; screen with plasma BK PCR; reduce immunosuppression.

q: A child with FSGS who receives a renal transplant has no risk of recurrence as the original kidneys are removed.
truth: ❌ TRAP — FSGS recurs in 30–50% post-transplant
exp: FSGS recurs in 30–50% of transplants due to circulating permeability factor. Nephrectomy of native kidneys does NOT prevent recurrence. Pre-emptive plasmapheresis may reduce risk.

q: A CMV D+/R− child post-renal transplant is stable. CMV prophylaxis is unnecessary as the child is asymptomatic.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Valganciclovir prophylaxis is mandatory for 3–6 months
exp: CMV D+/R− patients need valganciclovir prophylaxis for 3–6 months. Asymptomatic status does not negate the risk. CMV disease is a major cause of graft loss.

q: A child 3 months post-renal transplant is given MMR and varicella vaccines as part of routine immunisation.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Live vaccines are contraindicated post-transplant
exp: Live vaccines (MMR, varicella, BCG, yellow fever) are contraindicated post-transplant due to immunosuppression. Only inactivated vaccines are safe. Give live vaccines pre-transplant if possible.

q: A child with severe wasting and no oedema is diagnosed with kwashiorkor.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Wasting + no oedema = marasmus
exp: Marasmus: severe wasting, no oedema, 'skin and bones'. Kwashiorkor: oedema, hair changes, hepatomegaly, preserved subcutaneous fat. Confusing them = wrong management (kwashiorkor needs careful refeeding).

q: A child with SAM is alert and feeding. Blood glucose check is unnecessary on admission as the child is not lethargic.
truth: ❌ TRAP — ALWAYS check glucose on admission
exp: Hypoglycaemia is the most dangerous complication of SAM and can occur without warning signs. ALWAYS check and treat immediately on admission regardless of clinical appearance.

q: A child with SAM and dehydration is given standard WHO ORS for rehydration.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Use ReSoMal (reduced sodium ORS) for SAM
exp: Standard ORS has too much sodium for SAM. Use ReSoMal (reduced sodium ORS) or dilute standard ORS. Standard ORS in SAM risks hypernatraemia and heart failure.

q: A child with SAM is started on iron supplements in Phase 1 to correct anaemia.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Start iron ONLY in Phase 2 (after stabilisation)
exp: Iron in Phase 1 increases oxidative stress and infection risk. Start iron ONLY in Phase 2 (after oedema resolves and appetite returns). Phase 1 = antibiotics, F-75, micronutrients WITHOUT iron.

q: A child with SAM has no fever and no focal signs of infection. Antibiotics are unnecessary as there is no evidence of sepsis.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Give antibiotics to ALL SAM patients
exp: Give broad-spectrum antibiotics to ALL SAM patients even without obvious infection. Immune dysfunction masks signs of infection. Hypoglycaemia and hypothermia may be the only signs.

q: A child with SAM in Phase 1 is given high-protein F-100 therapeutic milk to promote rapid weight gain.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Phase 1 = F-75 LOW calorie; high-protein = refeeding syndrome
exp: Phase 1 = F-75 LOW calorie (75 kcal/100 mL). High-calorie/protein feeds in Phase 1 cause refeeding syndrome. Transition to F-100/RUTF only in Phase 2 after stabilisation.

q: A 2-year-old in the community is screened for malnutrition using weight-for-age only. MUAC is not a reliable indicator.
truth: ❌ TRAP — MUAC <11.5 cm is a standalone SAM criterion
exp: MUAC <11.5 cm is a standalone criterion for SAM in children 6–59 months. It is simple, reliable, and does not require scales or height boards. Use it for community screening.

q: A child with NS has no remission after 8 weeks of daily prednisolone. This is steroid-resistant NS.
truth: ❌ TRAP — SRNS = no remission at 4–6 weeks
exp: Steroid-resistant NS = no remission after 4 weeks of daily prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day (or 6 weeks total including alternate days). 8 weeks is too late — biopsy and CNI should have been started earlier.

q: A child with cirrhosis has no clinical signs of encephalopathy. This rules out hepatic encephalopathy completely.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Grade 0 (minimal HE) is diagnosed by psychometric testing
exp: Grade 0 (minimal HE) has no overt clinical signs but IS diagnosed by psychometric testing. Saying 'no HE' without testing is wrong. Minimal HE affects quality of life and driving safety.

q: A child with chronic hepatic encephalopathy is placed on long-term protein restriction to reduce ammonia production.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Never recommend long-term protein restriction in chronic HE
exp: Long-term protein restriction causes malnutrition and sarcopenia, which paradoxically worsen ammonia levels due to muscle wasting. Maintain 1–1.5 g/kg/day protein normally.

q: A 3-year-old with chronic constipation has no abdominal distension and passed meconium normally. This is Hirschsprung disease.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Normal meconium passage = functional constipation
exp: Hirschsprung: failure to pass meconium >48h, abdominal distension, tight empty rectum. Functional constipation: normal meconium, no distension, palpable stool in rectum. Confusing them = wrong diagnosis.

q: A jaundiced infant at 5 weeks has a normal ultrasound. Biliary atresia is ruled out.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Normal USG does NOT exclude biliary atresia
exp: USG may be normal in early biliary atresia. HIDA scan (no excretion) and liver biopsy (bile duct proliferation, portal fibrosis) are needed. Do NOT rely on USG alone to exclude BA.

q: A newborn with PKU has intellectual disability and seizures as the presenting features at birth.
truth: ❌ TRAP — PKU is asymptomatic at birth; features develop later
exp: PKU is asymptomatic at birth. Intellectual disability, seizures, and eczema develop if untreated. Newborn screening detects PKU before symptoms. Describing childhood features in a neonate = wrong.

q: A 16-year-old with PKU has normal IQ. The low-phenylalanine diet can be relaxed in adolescence.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Diet must be lifelong
exp: Low-phenylalanine diet must be LIFELONG. Relaxing the diet in adolescence causes cognitive decline, mood disorders, and executive dysfunction. Lifelong adherence is essential.

q: A child with PKU can safely consume diet sodas and sugar-free gums containing aspartame as part of a low-protein diet.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Aspartame contains phenylalanine and is contraindicated
exp: Aspartame is metabolised to phenylalanine. It is absolutely contraindicated in PKU. Families must read labels carefully and avoid all aspartame-containing products.

q: A newborn with PKU has intellectual disability and seizures as the presenting features at birth.
truth: ❌ TRAP — PKU is asymptomatic at birth; features develop later
exp: PKU is asymptomatic at birth. Intellectual disability, seizures, and eczema develop if untreated. Newborn screening detects PKU before symptoms. Describing childhood features in a neonate = wrong.

q: A newborn screened at 24 hours has a normal PKU result. No further testing is needed as the screen is negative.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Normal screen before 48h/before protein feeding does NOT rule out PKU
exp: Newborn screening for PKU requires protein feeding first (phenylalanine challenge) and testing after 48 hours. Testing before this can give false negatives. Must retest.

q: A newborn is screened for PKU at 36 hours after 2 feeds. This timing is adequate.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Screening must be after 48 hours AND after protein feeding
exp: Newborn screening for PKU requires testing AFTER 48 hours of age AND AFTER adequate protein feeding (phenylalanine challenge). Testing earlier or before feeding = false negatives.

q: A newborn of an HBsAg+ mother receives HBIG and vaccine at 18 hours after delivery.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Window is <12 hours, not 24
exp: HBIG + vaccine must be given within 12 hours of birth. Delay beyond 12 hours significantly reduces efficacy. 18 hours is too late — the window has closed.

q: A newborn receives HBIG and hepatitis B vaccine in the same syringe at the same injection site.
truth: ❌ TRAP — HBIG and vaccine must be at separate sites
exp: HBIG and hepatitis B vaccine must be given at different injection sites (different limbs). Same site = antibody neutralisation of vaccine = reduced efficacy.

q: An HBsAg+ mother is advised to stop breastfeeding to prevent perinatal transmission.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Breastfeeding is safe with proper prophylaxis
exp: Breastfeeding is safe if the infant receives HBIG + vaccine within 12 hours. Only contraindicated if mother has cracked/bleeding nipples. Do NOT routinely stop breastfeeding.

q: A premature infant weighing 1.8 kg born to an HBsAg+ mother receives standard HBIG + vaccine at birth only. No additional doses are needed.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Premature <2 kg infants need 4 doses total
exp: Premature infants <2 kg need 4 doses total: birth, 1 month, 2 months, and 6 months. The birth dose may not generate adequate response due to immature immune system.

q: Anti-HBs titres are checked at 1 month after the final vaccine dose.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Check anti-HBs 1–2 months after final dose
exp: Anti-HBs should be checked 1–2 months after the final vaccine dose (at 9–12 months of age), not immediately. Checking too early may give false negatives.

q: A newborn of an HBsAg+ mother receives HBIG and vaccine at 18 hours after delivery.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Window is <12 hours, not 24
exp: HBIG + vaccine must be given within 12 hours of birth. Delay beyond 12 hours significantly reduces efficacy. 18 hours is too late.

q: A child with hematuria, hypertension, and low C3 is diagnosed as urinary frequency.
truth: ❌ TRAP — This is polyuria, not urinary frequency
exp: Confusing polyuria with urinary frequency — polyuria = large volume; frequency = frequent small voids.

q: A child with polyuria and polydipsia has dilute urine with low specific gravity. This confirms diabetes insipidus without needing to check blood glucose.
truth: ❌ TRAP — DM is the #1 cause of polyuria; ALWAYS check glucose first
exp: Diabetes mellitus is the most common cause of polyuria in children. Always check blood glucose first. DI is a diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out DM, hypercalcaemia, and renal disease.

q: A child with hypernatraemic dehydration (Na⁺ 165) is corrected to Na⁺ 145 over 6 hours.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Correct hypernatraemia over 48–72 hours
exp: Rapid correction of hypernatraemia causes cerebral oedema and seizures. Correct over 48–72 hours. Max rate: 0.5 mEq/L/hr or 10–12 mEq/L/day. Faster = brain damage.

q: A child with nephrogenic DI is started on desmopressin (DDAVP) to reduce urine output.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Desmopressin does NOT work in nephrogenic DI
exp: Nephrogenic DI = ADH resistance at the collecting duct. Desmopressin (ADH analogue) does NOT work. Treatment: low-solute diet, thiazide diuretics, indomethacin, and amiloride.

q: Do not investigate for craniopharyngioma as the most common CNS tumor causing central DI in children as it rarely changes management.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Missing craniopharyngioma as the most common CNS tumor causing central DI in children is dangerous
exp: Missing craniopharyngioma as the most common CNS tumor causing central DI in children — always do MRI pituitary.

q: A female infant has nephrogenic DI. The X-linked form only affects males, so this must be an autosomal variant.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Female carriers of X-linked NDI can have mild defects
exp: X-linked nephrogenic DI (AVPR2 mutation) affects males primarily, but female carriers can have mild concentrating defects. Do not assume females are unaffected.

q: A child with nephrotic syndrome has a history of chronic NSAID use for arthritis. NSAIDs are the most common drug cause of nephrotic syndrome.
truth: ❌ TRAP — NSAIDs cause interstitial nephritis, not NS
exp: NSAIDs cause acute interstitial nephritis and minimal change disease (MCD), but they are NOT the most common drug cause of NS. Penicillamine, gold, and captopril are more classically associated with membranous nephropathy.
marks: Trap from Drugs Causing Nephrotic Syndrome

q: A child with Wilson's disease on penicillamine develops nephrotic syndrome. Penicillamine causes minimal change disease.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Penicillamine and gold cause membranous nephropathy
exp: Penicillamine and gold cause membranous nephropathy (not MCD). MCD is more commonly associated with NSAIDs and interferon. Know the drug-histology associations.
marks: Trap from Drugs Causing Nephrotic Syndrome
