q: A child with drug-induced nephrotic syndrome stops the offending drug. Proteinuria should resolve within 48 hours.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Proteinuria may take weeks to months to resolve
exp: Drug-induced nephrotic syndrome (especially membranous nephropathy) can take weeks to months to resolve after stopping the drug. Do not expect immediate resolution. Steroids may be needed.
marks: Trap from Drugs Causing Nephrotic Syndrome

q: A child on lithium for bipolar disorder develops polyuria and proteinuria. Lithium causes nephrogenic DI but not nephrotic syndrome.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Lithium causes BOTH nephrogenic DI AND nephrotic syndrome
exp: Lithium causes nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (ADH resistance) AND minimal change disease/nephrotic syndrome. Both are well-documented complications. Do not overlook either.
marks: Trap from Drugs Causing Nephrotic Syndrome

q: A child with osteogenesis imperfecta on pamidronate develops proteinuria. This is minimal change disease.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Pamidronate causes collapsing glomerulopathy/FSGS
exp: Bisphosphonates (pamidronate, zoledronate) cause collapsing glomerulopathy/FSGS in children with OI, not MCD. This is a high-yield association tested in exams.
marks: Trap from Drugs Causing Nephrotic Syndrome

q: A 3-year-old with first-episode NS and normal C3 is scheduled for renal biopsy before starting steroids.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Biopsy is NOT indicated in typical first-episode MCD
exp: Typical first-episode MCD (age 1–10, normal C3, no haematuria): start steroids WITHOUT biopsy. Biopsy before steroids is unnecessary and delays treatment. Biopsy only if atypical features.

q: A child with PSGN has low C3 at 4 weeks. Perform renal biopsy immediately to rule out MPGN.
truth: ❌ TRAP — C3 normalises by 6–8 weeks in PSGN
exp: C3 remains low for 6–8 weeks in PSGN. Biopsy at 4 weeks is premature. Wait until 8–12 weeks. If C3 still low beyond 8–12 weeks, THEN biopsy for MPGN.

q: A newborn with congenital NS is started on steroids immediately without biopsy.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Congenital NS is usually steroid-resistant and needs biopsy
exp: Congenital NS (Finnish type, diffuse mesangial sclerosis) is usually steroid-resistant. Biopsy is needed to establish diagnosis and guide management. Do NOT start steroids empirically.

q: A child with a solitary kidney and nephrotic syndrome is scheduled for percutaneous renal biopsy.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Solitary kidney is a relative contraindication
exp: Solitary kidney is a RELATIVE contraindication to percutaneous biopsy (risk of losing the only kidney). Open biopsy or careful consideration with experienced operator is needed.

q: A renal biopsy shows normal light microscopy. No further analysis is needed.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Light microscopy alone is insufficient
exp: Light microscopy alone is insufficient. Electron microscopy (EM) is needed for MCD (foot process effacement) and immune fluorescence for immune complex deposition. Always request EM + IF.

q: A child with acute pyelonephritis undergoes DMSA scan within 48 hours to assess for renal scarring.
truth: ❌ TRAP — DMSA for scarring must be deferred 3–6 months
exp: DMSA during acute infection shows acute pyelonephritis but CANNOT distinguish it from permanent scarring. Scarring assessment must be deferred to 3–6 months after infection. Early DMSA = misleading.

q: A 7-year-old girl with first uncomplicated UTI is scheduled for MCU to rule out VUR.
truth: ❌ TRAP — MCU is NOT first-line in older children with simple UTI
exp: MCU is invasive and not needed in every first UTI. Indicated in: <2 years with febrile UTI, recurrent UTIs, atypical organisms, poor urine stream, suspected PUV. Not for simple cystitis in older girls.

q: A 4-year-old with febrile UTI has no previous history. USG is not indicated as this is a first uncomplicated infection.
truth: ❌ TRAP — USG KUB is first-line for ALL children with febrile UTI
exp: USG KUB is first-line imaging for ALL children with febrile UTI — not just selected cases. It detects structural anomalies, hydronephrosis, and abscesses.

q: A child with first febrile UTI undergoes CT abdomen as first-line imaging.
truth: ❌ TRAP — CT is NOT first-line; USG KUB is first-line
exp: USG KUB is first-line for ALL children with febrile UTI. CT involves radiation and is reserved for complex cases (abscess, trauma, tumour suspicion). Never use CT as first-line for simple UTI.

q: A child with suspected obstructive uropathy is given DTPA to assess renal function and drainage.
truth: ❌ TRAP — MAG3 is preferred over DTPA in children
exp: MAG3 is preferred over DTPA in children because it is extracted more efficiently by the kidneys and provides better images with lower radiation. DTPA is less reliable in impaired renal function.

q: A child with acute pyelonephritis undergoes DMSA scan within 48 hours to assess for renal scarring.
truth: ❌ TRAP — DMSA for scarring must be deferred 3–6 months
exp: DMSA during acute infection shows acute pyelonephritis but CANNOT distinguish it from permanent scarring. Scarring assessment must be deferred to 3–6 months after infection. Early DMSA = misleading.

q: A child with adrenal insufficiency has hyperpigmentation and hyperkalemia. This indicates secondary adrenal insufficiency.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Hyperpigmentation + hyperkalemia = PRIMARY AI (Addison)
exp: Primary AI (Addison): hyperpigmentation (ACTH ↑) + hyperkalemia (aldosterone ↓). Secondary AI: NO hyperpigmentation, NO hyperkalemia. Getting this backwards loses marks.

q: A child with adrenal insufficiency has low ACTH. This confirms primary adrenal insufficiency.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Primary AI has ELEVATED ACTH
exp: Primary AI: low cortisol → high ACTH (feedback loop). Secondary AI: low cortisol → low ACTH (pituitary problem). ACTH levels distinguish primary from secondary.

q: A child with hypercortisolism from an adrenal adenoma is diagnosed with Cushing disease.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Cushing DISEASE = pituitary adenoma only
exp: Cushing DISEASE = pituitary adenoma (ACTH-dependent). Cushing SYNDROME = any cause of hypercortisolism (pituitary, adrenal, ectopic, exogenous). Adrenal adenoma = Cushing syndrome, NOT disease.

q: A child with Cushing syndrome has obesity and short stature. The obesity is the most important diagnostic clue in children.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Growth failure is the hallmark in children
exp: In children, growth failure (not obesity) is the hallmark of Cushing syndrome. Obese children are usually tall; short obese child with Cushing = growth arrest. This distinguishes pathological from simple obesity.

q: A child with suspected Cushing syndrome undergoes low-dose dexamethasone suppression test and cortisol suppresses. This rules out Cushing syndrome.
truth: ❌ TRAP — Suppression on low-dose dexamethasone = normal/obese
exp: Low-dose dexamethasone suppresses cortisol in normal and obese children but FAILS to suppress in Cushing syndrome. Failure to suppress = Cushing. Suppression = normal.

q: A child with Cushing syndrome has >50% cortisol suppression on high-dose dexamethasone. This confirms ectopic ACTH syndrome.
truth: ❌ TRAP — High-dose suppression = Cushing disease, NOT ectopic
exp: High-dose dexamethasone suppresses >50% in Cushing disease (pituitary) but NOT in ectopic ACTH (autonomous). No suppression = ectopic. >50% suppression = pituitary/Cushing disease.

q: A 22 kg child with PSGN needs Strep eradication. Give Benzathine Penicillin G 600,000 U IM as a single dose.
truth: ✓ CORRECT — Weight <27 kg → 600,000 U
exp: The exact threshold is 27 kg. Below = 600K U. At or above = 1.2 million U. Always state the weight threshold explicitly in the exam.

q: A child has haematuria with normal C3, normal C4, and onset 2 days after a throat infection. Diagnose IgA nephropathy.
truth: ✓ CORRECT — Short latent + normal C3 = IgA
exp: IgA nephropathy: synpharyngitic (1–3 days), normal complement. PSGN: latent 10–21 days, low C3. Timing + C3 distinguish them.
marks: Normal C3 + haematuria = IgA, not PSGN

q: First episode NS in a 4-year-old: start Prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg/day) for 6 weeks.
truth: ✓ CORRECT — Phase 1 ISKD protocol
exp: 2 mg/kg/day × 6 weeks is Phase 1. Max cap 60 mg regardless of weight. Then 1.5 mg/kg alternate days × 6 weeks = total 12 weeks.
marks: +1M for correct dose, max, and duration

q: Serum C3 is low in a child presenting with first-episode NS. Perform a renal biopsy before starting steroids.
truth: ✓ CORRECT — Low C3 = biopsy first
exp: Low C3 suggests MPGN or membranous GN, not MCD. Starting steroids on MPGN without biopsy gives false response data. Biopsy before steroids if C3 is low.
marks: Low C3 + NS = biopsy, not empirical steroids

q: NS child with cold peripheries, tachycardia, and hypotension (hypovolaemia): give 25% albumin 0.5–1.0 g/kg IV.
truth: ✓ CORRECT — Albumin for hypovolaemia
exp: Hypovolaemia despite oedema is the paradox of NS. Signs: cold peripheries, tachycardia, low BP. Treatment: 25% albumin 0.5–1.0 g/kg IV over 1–2 h + loop diuretic after.

q: Serum K⁺ = 3.0 mEq/L in a child with DKA before insulin is started. Hold insulin and correct potassium first.
truth: ✓ CORRECT — K⁺ <3.3 = hold insulin
exp: Insulin drives K⁺ intracellularly. If already hypokalaemic (K⁺ <3.3), starting insulin causes fatal arrhythmia. Must correct K⁺ first, then start insulin when ≥3.3.

q: Blood glucose in DKA falls to 210 mg/dL. Add dextrose to IV fluids and continue the insulin infusion.
truth: ✓ CORRECT — Add dextrose, keep insulin running
exp: Insulin must continue until acidosis resolves (pH >7.30, HCO₃ >18), not just until glucose normalises. Add dextrose to prevent hypoglycaemia while ketosis clears.

q: DKA child develops headache, bradycardia, and altered consciousness. Give Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV immediately.
truth: ✓ CORRECT — Cerebral oedema protocol
exp: Classic cerebral oedema triad. Treatment: Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV OR 3% NaCl 5–10 mL/kg, reduce fluid rate, elevate head 30°. Both mannitol and 3% NaCl are valid.

q: STEC-confirmed HUS: withhold all antibiotics and manage supportively.

q: Starting eculizumab for aHUS: vaccinate against Neisseria meningitidis before the first dose.

q: Urine microscopy shows RBC casts. This is pathognomonic for glomerular haematuria.
truth: ✓ CORRECT — RBC casts = glomerular, always
exp: RBC casts form when RBCs trapped in Tamm-Horsfall protein in the tubules — only possible if RBCs enter at the glomerular level. Pathognomonic = glomerular until proven otherwise.
